html:
[1]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 埋忧集 - Title for a collection of writings, literally 'a collection to bury worries,' suggesting a theme of solace or reflection.
  • 续集 - Means 'continuation' or 'sequel,' indicating that this is a follow-up volume to the original work.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 清人 - Refers to a person from the Qing dynasty (1644–1912), specifying the time period of the author.
  • 朱翊清 - As a Qing dynasty writer, Zhu Yiqing's works are part of the broader literary tradition of the time, which valued classical Chinese and often incorporated moral or philosophical themes.
  • 目录 - The term '目录' (mùlù) refers to a table of contents, a common feature in classical Chinese texts.

Proper Nouns

  • 《埋忧集》 - The title of the collection, which should be rendered as 'Burial of Woes' in English, though the literal translation is retained for clarity.
  • 朱翊清 - The author's name, consistently rendered as 'Zhu Yiqing.'

[2]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 奇獄 - Unusual or perplexing legal cases, often involving supernatural or extraordinary elements.
  • 谲判 - Clever or unconventional approach to resolving legal cases, often involving a twist or unexpected outcome.
  • 夫妇重逢 - Reunion between spouses, often involving separation or loss.
  • 海大鱼 - Person of great significance or influence, often used metaphorically.
  • 车夫 - Person who drives a cart or carriage, often a common laborer.
  • 奇儿 - Child with unusual characteristics or abilities, often involving supernatural elements.
  • 贾义士 - Person of high moral standing, often involved in acts of righteousness.
  • 姚三公子 - Specific individual, often involved in significant events or stories.
  • 严侍郎 - High-ranking official, often involved in important decisions or events.
  • 星卜 - Fortune-telling using celestial bodies, often involving predictions or divinations.
  • 常开平遗枪 - Gun left by Mr. Chang Kaiping, often involving historical or personal significance.
  • 人面豆 - Bean with a human face, often used metaphorically or in folk tales.
  • 奎光 - Qiu's light, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 陈学士 - Mr. Chen, the scholar, often involved in academic or literary pursuits.
  • 徐孝子 - Mr. Xu, the filial son, often involved in acts of filial piety.
  • 男妾 - Male concubine, often involving social or historical context.
  • 上智潭鼋 - Turtle in Shangzhi Spring, often involving historical or cultural significance.
  • 武松墓 - Wu Song's tomb, often involving historical or literary references.
  • 死经三次 - Died three times, often involving supernatural or extraordinary elements.
  • 宅异 - Strange house, often involving supernatural or unusual occurrences.
  • 柜中熊 - Bear in the cabinet, often involving strange or unusual objects.
  • 遗米化珠梦庐先生遗事 - Story of Mr. Meng Lu, who dreamed of rice turning into pearls, often involving folk tales or legends.
  • 捐官 - Buying an official position, often involving historical or social context.
  • 辨诬 - Clearing a slander, often involving legal or social issues.
  • 荷花公主 - Lotus Princess, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 夜叉 - Demon, often involving supernatural or mythological elements.
  • 奇疾 - Strange disease, often involving unexplained or unusual illnesses.
  • 真生 - True Life, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 明季遗事 - Events of the late Ming period, often involving historical context.
  • 树中人 - Person in the tree, often involving folk tales or legends.
  • 陈忠愍公死难事 - Story of Mr. Chen Zhongmian's martyrdom, often involving historical events.
  • 乌桕树 - Chinese tallow tree, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 狮子 - Lion, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 谄效 - Flattery and imitation, often involving social or historical context.
  • 醉和尚 - Drunken monk, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 香树尚书全荃 - Mr. Quanquan, the Perfumed Tree Shangshu, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 周烂鼻 - Mr. Zhou, the one with the rotten nose, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 潘烂头 - Mr. Pan, the one with the rotten head, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 臀痒 - Itchy buttocks, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 草庵和尚 - Monk of the thatched hut, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 樊恼 - Mr. Fan, the one who causes annoyance, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 许真君 - Mr. Xu, the True Lord, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 茅山道人憎须 - Taoist of Mount Mao, who hates his mustache, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 梁山州 - Liangshan Prefecture, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 诗嘲 - Poetic ridicule, often involving literary or social context.
  • 陶公轶事 - Extraordinary events of Mr. Tao, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 改名 - Changing one's name, often involving historical or cultural context.
  • 负债鬼 - Ghost of a debtor, often involving supernatural or mythological elements.
  • 蛇异 - Strange snake, often involving folk tales or legends.
  • 佛时 - Buddha's time, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 贞观 - Zhenguan era, often involving historical context.
  • 剪舌 - Cutting the tongue, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 续集卷一 - Volume One of the Sequel, often involving literary or historical context.
  • 刘綎 - Mr. Liu Teng, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 黄石斋 - Mr. Huang, the Yellow Stone Pavilion, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 对缢 - Hanging together, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 生祭 - Living sacrifice, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 熊襄愍轶事 - Extraordinary events of Mr. Xiong Xiangmin, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 地震 - Earthquake, often involving natural disasters or historical events.
  • 王秋泉 - Mr. Wang Qiuquan, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 蚺蛇 - King cobra, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 采龙眼 - Picking longan fruit, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 大言 - Grand words, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 陆世科 - Mr. Lu Shike, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 猩猩 - Orangutan, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 燕妒 - Jealousy of the swallow, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 戒贪 - Warning against greed, often involving moral or ethical lessons.
  • 师戒 - Teacher's warning, often involving moral or ethical lessons.
  • 牡丹 - Peony, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 柳画 - Willow painting, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 湖市 - Lake market, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 冰山录 - Ice Mountain Record, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 泰山 - Mount Tai, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 夷俗 - Barbarian customs, often involving cultural or historical context.
  • 双林凌氏 - The Ling family of Shuanglin, often involving historical or cultural references.
  • 杨园先生水月庵 - Mr. Yang Yuan's Water Moon Hermitage, often involving historical or cultural references.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 腹语 - Could refer to ventriloquism or a form of communication through the abdomen; exact meaning unclear.
  • 熊伯龙 - Likely a proper name, possibly of a historical figure or a person of significance.
  • 库中画 - Could mean 'paintings in the treasury' or 'stored paintings'; exact meaning uncertain.
  • 乩书 - Refers to writings produced by a medium during a séance, often associated with spiritual or supernatural communication.
  • 玉人 - Could mean 'jade person' or 'beautiful person'; exact meaning depends on context.
  • 大胆 - Means 'brave' or 'reckless'; exact connotation depends on context.
  • 项王走马埒 - Likely refers to a historical event involving Xiang Wang and a racecourse.
  • 无支祈 - A mythological creature, possibly a water demon or a monster.
  • 人面疮 - Literally 'face with sores'; possibly a metaphor for a person with a disfigured or pockmarked face.
  • 陈句山 - Likely a proper name, possibly of a historical figure or a person of significance.
  • 瘗蚕 - Could refer to the burial of silkworms or a ritual involving silkworms.
  • 偿债犬 - Literally 'dog that repays debts'; possibly a metaphor for a loyal or indebted dog.
  • 剥皮 - Means 'skinning' or 'flaying'; often used in the context of punishment or torture.
  • 仙方 - Refers to magical or alchemical formulas, often associated with Daoist practices.
  • 耿通 - Likely a proper name, possibly of a historical figure or a person of significance.
  • 陆忠毅公传赞异兽 - Likely refers to a biography or praise of a person (Lu Zhongyi) and a mention of an unusual creature.
  • 殿试卷 - Refers to the exam papers written by candidates for the imperial examinations.
  • 推背图 - A famous Chinese divination text, often associated with predicting the future.
  • 李自成 - A historical figure, the leader of the rebel forces during the Ming-Qing transition.
  • 徐珠渊 - Likely a proper name, possibly of a historical figure or a person of significance.
  • 毛文龙传辨 - Likely refers to a biography or analysis of Mao Wénlóng, a historical figure.
  • 自序 - Means 'self-introduction' or 'preface'; often used to introduce the author's background or intentions.

[4]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 春归里门 - Literally 'spring returns to the village gate,' used to describe the author's return to his hometown during spring.
  • 同人 - Refers to fellow scholars or friends.
  • 谓可以问世 - Means 'think it can be published,' indicating the group's opinion on the manuscript's publication.
  • 醵金 - Means 'collect money,' referring to the act of raising funds.
  • 付梓 - Means 'to print,' specifically referring to the process of publishing a book.
  • 剞劂事 - Refers to the work of engraving, specifically the preparation of printing blocks.
  • 没世之感 - Means 'a feeling of being forgotten after death,' expressing the author's concern about being forgotten.
  • 老矣 - Means 'old now,' indicating the author's age.
  • 追忆 - Means 'to recall,' referring to the act of remembering past events.
  • 以有用之居诸 - Means 'to use one's time,' referring to the author's use of his time.
  • 供无聊之歌哭 - Means 'to provide for aimless singing and crying,' describing the author's activities as futile.
  • 身世 - Refers to one's life and status.
  • 良月 - Means 'good month,' referring to the month of August.
  • 归安 - A place name in Zhejiang Province, China.
  • 梅叔氏 - The author's courtesy name, Zhuyiqing, also known as Meishu.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 道光二十五年 - Refers to the 25th year of the Daoguang era, which is 1845 AD.
  • 乙巳 - The stem-branch calendar system, where '乙巳' corresponds to the year 1845.
  • 归安 - A place name in Zhejiang Province, China.
  • 浔溪 - A place name, likely a river or stream near the author's residence.
  • 寓舍 - Refers to a temporary residence or study.

Proper Nouns

  • 道光二十五年 - The 25th year of the Daoguang era, 1845 AD.
  • 乙巳 - The stem-branch calendar system, corresponding to 1845.
  • 归安 - A place name in Zhejiang Province, China.
  • 浔溪 - A place name, likely a river or stream near the author's residence.
  • 寓舍 - A temporary residence or study.
  • 朱翊清 - The author's name, also known as Meishu.
  • 竹屏蒋君 - A specific person who took on the task of engraving.

[5]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 康熙間 - Period during the reign of Emperor Kangxi of the Qing dynasty.
  • 勾曲道士忘筌 - Name of a Taoist priest from the Gouqu region, whose name is 'Wangquan.'
  • 本武昌名家子 - Indicates that he was originally from a prominent family in Wuchang.
  • 以幼孤避亂，入道勞山 - Describes his early life, being orphaned and seeking refuge in Taoism on Laoshan Mountain.
  • 性豪逸，耽書嗜飲，善畫墨竹 - Describes his personality and interests, being bold and free-spirited, fond of reading and drinking, and skilled in painting bamboo.
  • 尤精于琴 - Indicates his particular expertise in playing the guqin.
  • 遇良材，必重價購之，至於典質不倦 - Describes his dedication to acquiring high-quality guqin materials, even going as far as mortgaging his possessions.
  • 新安吳商名畏龍者 - Refers to a merchant named Wu Wei Long from Xianan (modern-day Anhui).
  • 蓄琴頗富 - Indicates that he has a large collection of guqins.
  • 裹糧往訪 - Describes the action of packing food and traveling to visit someone.
  • 煉士亦善此乎 - A polite way of asking if the Taoist is also skilled in playing the guqin.
  • 固生平所好也 - Means 'it is indeed my lifelong passion.'
  • 但恨未遇名材耳 - Expresses regret for not having encountered famous guqin materials.
  • 此宋賈相悅生堂中物 - Refers to a guqin that was once owned by the Song dynasty official Jia Xiang.
  • 向以五百金購得之 - Indicates that it was purchased for 500 taels of silver.
  • 然亦非上品 - Suggests that although it is valuable, it is not the highest quality.
  • 聞先生多蓄古琴 - Indicates that the merchant has many ancient guqins.
  • 故不憚遠涉 - Expresses that he did not hesitate to travel a long distance.
  • 未識可賜一觀否 - A polite way of asking if the merchant could allow him to see one of his guqins.
  • 商與論琴理 - Describes the conversation about the theory of guqin playing.
  • 筌為細述勾撥挑剔之法 - Describes the detailed explanation of the techniques of playing the guqin.
  • 語多神解 - Suggests that the explanations were profound and insightful.
  • 商一時未能盡領 - Indicates that the merchant did not fully understand all of the explanations.
  • 請傳之妙手 - Asks for the Taoist to demonstrate his skill.
  • 解囊，為彈《水仙操》一闋 - Describes the Taoist taking out his guqin and playing the piece 'Shuixian Cao.'
  • 危坐竦聽 - Describes the merchant sitting upright and listening attentively.
  • 如有山林杳冥、海濤汩沒起於座中 - A vivid description of the music, suggesting that it evoked the imagery of distant mountains, forests, and the sound of waves.
  • 輒為歎絕 - Indicates that the merchant was deeply impressed by the performance.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 康熙間 - The reign period of Emperor Kangxi (1662-1722) in the Qing dynasty.
  • 勾曲道士忘筌 - A Taoist priest from the Gouqu region, whose name is 'Wangquan.'
  • 武昌名家子 - Indicates that he was from a prominent family in Wuchang, a city in modern-day Hubei.
  • 勞山 - Laoshan Mountain, a famous Taoist mountain in Shandong.
  • 宋賈相悅生堂中物 - Refers to a guqin that was once owned by the Song dynasty official Jia Xiang.
  • 新安吳商名畏龍者 - A merchant named Wu Wei Long from Xianan (modern-day Anhui).
  • 《水仙操》 - A well-known guqin piece, often associated with the story of the Water Nymph.

Proper Nouns

  • 康熙 - The reign title of Emperor Kangxi.
  • 勾曲 - A region in China.
  • 忘筌 - The name of the Taoist priest.
  • 武昌 - A city in modern-day Hubei.
  • 勞山 - Laoshan Mountain.
  • 宋賈相 - A Song dynasty official.
  • 新安 - A region in modern-day Anhui.
  • 吳商名畏龍 - The name of the merchant.
  • 《水仙操》 - A guqin piece.

[6]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 筌停琴，言曰 - Quan paused his zither and said.
  • 此调自伯牙传至嵇康，名《广陵散》，所谓观涛广陵者也 - This tune was passed down from Boya to Jikang, named 'Guangling San,' which refers to watching waves at Guangling.
  • 康死，此调已绝，某特以意谱之耳 - After Jikang's death, this tune was lost, and I have composed it anew based on my understanding.
  • 商乃出其素所珍藏者十余琴，皆不足观 - Shang then took out over ten of his cherished zithers, but all were not worth seeing.
  • 筌爱玩不忍释，请以所携琴易之，不许，增以五百金，亦不许，呼仆取入 - Quan loved it so much he couldn't put it down and asked to exchange his own zither for it. Shang refused, and even when Quan offered 500 gold, Shang still refused. He then called a servant to take it inside.
  • 筌乃起，怅然而出，谋诸阍者 - Quan then got up, left in disappointment, and sought advice from the gatekeeper.
  • 今见师赏鉴若此，岂复能动以利乎 - Now that you see the master appreciates it so much, how could he be moved by profit?
  • 筌乃出，赁居一僧寺，誓不得琴不返，然卒无可为计，惟日饮 - Quan then left, rented a room in a temple, vowed not to return without the zither, but ultimately had no plan, and just drank every day.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 伯牙 - Boya, a legendary musician from the Spring and Autumn period, known for his friendship with Ziqi.
  • 嵇康 - Jikang, a famous philosopher and musician from the Three Kingdoms period, known for his music and writings.
  • 《广陵散》 - 'Guangling San,' a famous ancient Chinese musical piece, often associated with Jikang.
  • 观涛广陵 - 'Watching waves at Guangling,' a poetic description of the location where the tune was said to have been played.
  • 金猫睛 - Gold cat's eye, a type of precious stone.
  • 龙肝石 - Dragon liver stone, a type of stone believed to have special properties.
  • 穿云 - 'Piercing the clouds,' a poetic term often used in Chinese poetry to describe something extraordinary or transcendent.
  • 风雅 - Refinement and elegance, often associated with high culture and the arts.

Proper Nouns

  • 筌 - Quan, the character who paused his zither and spoke.
  • 商 - Shang, the character who owns the zithers.
  • 伯牙 - Boya, the legendary musician.
  • 嵇康 - Jikang, the famous philosopher and musician.
  • 《广陵散》 - 'Guangling San,' the famous musical piece.
  • 金猫睛 - Gold cat's eye, a type of precious stone.
  • 龙肝石 - Dragon liver stone, a type of stone.
  • 穿云 - 'Piercing the clouds,' a poetic term.
  • 风雅 - Refinement and elegance.

[7]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 無何 - Temporal adverb meaning 'not long after' or 'soon.'
  • 對月獨酌 - Poetic expression for solitary contemplation, literally 'drinking alone under the moon.'
  • 資用將竭 - Means 'resources are about to run out,' indicating financial or material scarcity.
  • 寶琴終不可得 - Means 'the precious qin (a type of Chinese zither) will never be obtained,' expressing unfulfilled desire.
  • 凄然泣下 - Means 'sadly wept,' conveying deep emotional sorrow.
  • 忽聞牆陰屟響有聲 - Means 'suddenly heard a sound of footsteps in the shadow of the wall,' indicating an unexpected arrival.
  • 豐姿綽約 - Means 'graceful and charming appearance,' describing a beautiful woman.
  • 清歌侑酒 - Means 'singing to accompany the wine,' a traditional form of entertainment.
  • 破岑寂 - Means 'to break the silence,' suggesting relief from loneliness.
  • 可乎 - Polite interrogative meaning 'is it acceptable?' or 'is it okay?'
  • 勿勞窮詰 - Means 'do not trouble to question further,' indicating a request for discretion.
  • 當非禍君者 - Means 'it is not someone who will bring you misfortune,' a reassurance.
  • 黃牙拍板 - Refers to a musical instrument, specifically a type of clapper used in traditional Chinese music.
  • 音韻凄婉 - Means 'melodious and mournful,' describing the emotional quality of the music.
  • 顧盼生姿 - Means 'her gaze and glances create charm,' describing the woman's captivating presence.
  • 竟醉倒於床上 - Means 'finally fell asleep on the bed,' indicating a state of deep intoxication.
  • 窗中斜月莹然矣 - Means 'the slanted moon was shining through the window,' indicating the passage of time.
  • 妾亦非私奔者 - Means 'I am not a runaway,' a statement of loyalty.
  • 自蒙青盼 - Means 'since you have favored me,' indicating a relationship of mutual affection.
  • 覺人間尚有中郎 - Means 'I feel there are still people like Zhong Rang in the world,' a reference to a historical figure known for his integrity and talent.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 寶琴 - Refers to a precious qin (a type of Chinese zither), symbolizing high cultural value and artistic pursuit.
  • 屟響 - Refers to the sound of footsteps, often associated with the soft, light steps of a woman.
  • 黃牙拍板 - A traditional Chinese musical instrument, often used in folk performances.
  • 琴心 - A term often used in classical Chinese literature to refer to a piece of music or a song, often associated with the qin (Chinese zither).
  • 中郎 - Refers to Zhong Rang, a historical figure known for his integrity and talent, often used as a metaphor for a virtuous and talented person.

Proper Nouns

  • 中郎 - Refers to Zhong Rang, a historical figure known for his integrity and talent.

[8]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 情深如许 - Literary expression meaning 'having known the depth of your feelings.'
  • 背主而来 - Means 'therefore, leaving one's master to come,' indicating a departure from a previous allegiance.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '又有某甲素嗜博，已倾其家' introduces the main character and his habit of gambling, which has led to the loss of his family's wealth.
  • The clause '后其妻病将死，谓甲曰' sets up the dialogue between the wife and the husband, with the wife being on the verge of death.
  • The wife's statement '余病至此，设有不测，身无寸缕，奈何' is a plea for her husband to provide her with clothing before she dies.
  • The husband's response '今烟火屡绝，乞贷无门，汝不见吾之悬鹑百结，而能顾汝乎' is a harsh rejection, indicating that he is too poor to provide for her.
  • The phrase '不顾而去' indicates the husband's indifference and lack of compassion.
  • The wife dies after a great cry, emphasizing the emotional impact of the husband's rejection.
  • The wife's family arrives with a few items of clothing and prepares for her burial.
  • The husband returns home and is pleased to see the preparations for his wife's burial.
  • The husband goes out to gamble again and loses all his money.
  • The husband returns home feeling ashamed and angry, unable to come up with a plan.
  • The husband goes to the burial place and secretly opens the coffin.
  • The wife suddenly sits up, shocking the husband and causing him to run away.
  • The husband hears the sound of wooden shoes approaching and sees his wife walking slowly towards him, causing him to run home and lock the door.
  • The husband is terrified and runs home, but finds his wife's body lying on the ground. He laughs at the body and drags it back to the burial place, stripping it of all clothing and placing it in the coffin before fleeing.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 明季余乡多土寇 - This sentence introduces the setting and the threat of bandits.
  • 乡民某，妻名雪姑，素贞静，事姑尤孝 - This sentence introduces the main character and her virtues.
  • 一日其夫出，有土寇入姑室 - This sentence describes the incident where the bandit enters the house.
  • 姑度不能免，引刃自刺，血溅寇衣 - This sentence describes the mother-in-law's attempt to resist the bandit.
  • 刃夺去，不得死，竟为所辱 - This sentence describes the failure of the mother-in-law's attempt and the tragic outcome.
  • 姑觇刃在旁，突取击寇，中其股 - This sentence describes the mother-in-law's quick thinking and bravery.
  • 寇大恨，裸其衣，以刃刺下体，穿穴而死 - This sentence describes the bandit's violent reaction and the mother-in-law's death.
  • 或仿世修降表李家例，题其门曰“穿臀李家” - This sentence describes the public humiliation of the family.
  • 其家人皆以为耻，而不复言 - This sentence describes the family's reaction to the incident.
  • 噫！是何弗思之甚也！ - This sentence expresses the author's criticism of the family's lack of understanding.
  • 夫家人子妇，一朝被劫，而其后遂为逐水之桃花者有矣 - This sentence describes the common fate of women who are defiled.
  • 彼雪姑既已受玷，则此耻虽引西江之水亦不能灌矣 - This sentence emphasizes the depth of the shame.
  • 使未能励操雅于平昔，何能奋死不顾若是？ - This sentence questions the mother-in-law's earlier virtues and her bravery.
  • 特不幸而为贼所辱耳 - This sentence expresses sympathy for the mother-in-law's plight.
  • 然其志可哀也，有志风化者，犹当表而出之，以与费宫娥刺虎并传 - This sentence suggests that the mother-in-law's bravery should be recognized and praised.
  • 而当事置之，乡之人笑之，即其家亦且讳之 - This sentence criticizes the lack of recognition and the community's reaction.
  • 人心之不明，乃至是非颠倒如是！ - This sentence criticizes the moral blindness of society.
  • 余故表而出之，以愧世之为河间妇者 - This sentence concludes with the author's intention to recognize the mother-in-law's bravery and shame those who are morally corrupt.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence describes a sequence of events: after several days, the woman and the man composed poetry together under the lamp, with the maid standing by.
  • The man pointed to the maid and said something.
  • This rhetorical question suggests that despite destiny being predetermined, without this person, there would be no such moment.
  • The woman laughed lightly and said something.
  • A rhetorical question asking how to repay kindness.
  • The man remained silent but looked at the maid and smiled.
  • The woman immediately stopped composing and ordered another maid to bring a lamp and bedding, leading the man to the maid's bed.
  • The maid was too shy to look up, and the woman hurriedly pulled the maids along.
  • After entering the room and lying down, the maid whispered something.
  • The maid wondered if this night's encounter could be something one could dream of.
  • The maid expressed excitement and nervousness.
  • The man modestly stated that he was old but this was how he repaid the woman's kindness, and he could not avoid being bold.
  • The act of consummating the relationship was described, and then it stopped.
  • The man had numerous lovers and no longer chased after fleeting desires.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 外史氏曰 - Conventional opening used by historians or commentators; can be translated as 'The historian says' or 'The commentator remarks.'
  • 右为余外高祖前邱吴长庚太史所记 - Formal statement indicating the text is a record by the author's great-great-grandfather, Wu Changgeng, a historian. '右' can be translated as 'the following' or 'the text below.'
  • 篇中纯用散叙，简核错落 - Describes the style of the text as straightforward narration with concise and varied sentences. '纯用散叙' can be translated as 'purely narrative,' and '简核错落' as 'concise and varied.'
  • 文之妙在于能碎 - Suggests that the beauty of the writing lies in its fragmented or broken structure. '能碎' can be translated as 'fragmented' or 'broken.'
  • 非昌黎以下所及也 - Indicates that this style surpasses the works of Han Yu (昌黎) and those who followed him. '昌黎' refers to Han Yu, a famous Tang dynasty writer and philosopher.
  • 若邵公能知前世事，固奇矣 - Suggests that if Shao Gong could know past events, it would be extraordinary. '固奇矣' can be translated as 'indeed, it would be strange.'
  • 然其事世亦闻有之矣 - Indicates that such stories are also heard in the world. '世亦闻有之矣' can be translated as 'indeed, such stories are also heard in the world.'
  • 至吕夫人则又能知三世以后事 - Suggests that Lady Lu could know events three generations into the future. '三世以后' can be translated as 'three generations into the future.'
  • 为问古来传奇中，有此创闻否 - Asks whether such a story has ever been heard before in legends. '创闻' can be translated as 'unheard' or 'novel.'
  • 题目既奇，文安得不奇 - Suggests that if the subject is strange, the text must also be strange. '安得不奇' can be translated as 'how could it not be strange.'
  • 于是仆本恨人，惊心不已 - Indicates that the speaker, who is already full of sorrow, is deeply moved. '仆本恨人' can be translated as 'I, who am already full of sorrow.'
  • 读之，始悟古今所谓慧业仙人，无非所谓情种也 - Suggests that after reading, one realizes that what is called 'wise and virtuous people' are simply 'people of deep emotion.' '慧业仙人' can be translated as 'wise and virtuous people,' and '情种' as 'people of deep emotion.'
  • 我欲将此文献之月下老翁 - Indicates that the speaker wants to present this text to the Moon God (月下老翁), who is a deity associated with matchmaking. '月下老翁' can be translated as 'the Moon God' or 'the Matchmaker God.'
  • 乞其广牖灵根，以补离恨之天 - Suggests that the speaker asks the Moon God to expand his spiritual roots to fill the sky of separation and sorrow. '广牖灵根' can be translated as 'expand his spiritual roots,' and '离恨之天' as 'the sky of separation and sorrow.'
  • 俾天下有情人，世世都成眷属 - Suggests that the speaker hopes that all people in love will always be united. '俾' can be translated as 'that' or 'so that,' and '世世都成眷属' as 'always be united.'
  • 老翁其许我乎 - Asks whether the Moon God will agree to the speaker's request. '其许我乎' can be translated as 'will he agree to my request?'

  • 书毕，拂袖竟归 - After writing, he brushed his sleeves and returned home. This describes the artist's dignified departure after completing his work.
  • 后太守至，见之大惊 - Later, when the prefect arrived, he was greatly surprised. This shows the prefect's reaction upon seeing the painting.
  • 从一仆亲造其庐，酬以百金 - He sent a servant to personally visit his house and offered a hundred gold pieces as compensation. This describes the prefect's attempt to appease the artist.
  • 先秦辞曰 - Xian Qin said. This introduces the artist's response to the prefect's offer.
  • 性不知暑，故无需此物也 - I do not feel the heat, so I do not need this thing. This is a humble and polite refusal of the prefect's offer.
  • 乃止，委金而去 - So he stopped, left the gold, and went away. This describes the prefect's departure after the artist's refusal.
  • 先秦追掷之，不及，乃返，投置败簏中，终不复顾 - Xian Qin chased after him, but could not catch up, so he returned, threw it into a broken basket, and never looked back. This describes the artist's reaction to the gold, showing his disdain for material wealth.
  • 数月，其金化为蝴蝶，一一飞去 - After several months, the gold turned into butterflies, one by one flying away. This is a fantastical element, symbolizing the artist's spiritual transformation and the futility of material wealth.
  • 崇祯时，孙公传庭柿园之役 - During the reign of Emperor Chongzhen, General Sun Chuanjing's campaign at Shizi Garden. This provides the historical context for the second part of the text.
  • 以帝命监军御史苏京促战而败 - Following the emperor's order, the military inspector Su Jing urged battle, leading to defeat. This describes the cause of the defeat.
  • 幕客某谓之曰 - A certain guest in the tent said to him. This introduces the advisor's advice to General Sun.
  • 昨余昼寝，见有人皆长尺余，披铠持矛，乘车装马，自陷中出，乘几登灶 - Yesterday, while I was napping during the day, I saw people all about a foot tall, wearing armor and holding spears, riding in chariots and horses, emerging from the battlefield, climbing onto the table and the stove. This is a dream vision that foreshadows the disaster.
  • 蒋山道士朱应子，令作沸汤，浇所入处 - The Taoist priest Zhu Yingzi from Mount Jiangshan ordered boiling water to be poured where they entered. This describes the attempt to neutralize the small people.
  • 因掘之，有斛许大蚁死穴中 - They dug and found a large number of dead ants about the size of a dou (a unit of volume) in the hole. This is the result of the investigation, suggesting that the small people were actually ants.
  • 乃叹曰 - Then he sighed and said. This introduces General Sun's reflection on the incident.
  • 吾误听道士，遂以儿戏杀百万生灵 - I mistakenly listened to the Taoist priest, and thus killed millions of lives in a child's game. This is General Sun's realization of the gravity of the mistake.
  • 彼其持矛登几时，非俨然从军出塞者乎 - When they held their spears and climbed onto the table, were they not clearly soldiers going out to the frontier? This is a rhetorical question that emphasizes the seriousness of the situation.
  • 孙公大哭 - General Sun cried bitterly. This describes his emotional response to the realization of the disaster.

  • 昨蒙宠贶，内有诗韵四册，及所题之字，皆未识命意所在 - This sentence is elliptical and requires interpretation. It means 'Last night, I received your favor, which included four volumes of poetry rhymes and the inscriptions on them, but I do not understand the intention behind them.'
  • 无他。诗韵者平上去入而已，‘之于于归’，自应是平上去入矣 - This response is cryptic and relies on wordplay. It means 'There is no other meaning. The poetry rhymes are simply the four tones (平上去入), and ‘之于于归’ naturally corresponds to them.'
  • 昨宵亲母太多情，为看花袍绕膝行 - This line is a playful poem, with the first line meaning 'Last night, my mother-in-law was overly affectionate, as she looked at the flowered robe and walked around the knees.'
  • 没有了。诗如何无结句？ - This exchange is humorous, with the first line meaning 'There is no more,' and the second asking 'How can a poem have no closing line?' The response is a joke about the poetic meter.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 而任為夜不收數年 - Indicates that the person named '任' served in the role of '夜不收' (night patrol or night guard) for several years.
  • 以罪除粮 - Suggests that the person was punished by having their rations taken away due to some offense.
  • 日纵博大嚼 - Means 'daily indulging in gambling and eating,' indicating a lifestyle of excess.
  • 靴笠偿酒家资 - '靴笠' refers to boots and hats, which are used here as collateral to pay off debts to the tavern.
  • 无所投 - Means 'nowhere to go' or 'no place to turn,' indicating a state of destitution.
  • 日拥败絮 - Means 'daily huddling in rags,' describing a state of poverty.
  • 空腹卧榻上 - Means 'lying on the bed with an empty stomach,' indicating hunger and lack of resources.
  • 无面出见人 - Means 'ashamed to face people,' indicating a state of social disgrace.
  • 一日偶出门不归 - Means 'one day, he happened to leave home and did not return,' indicating a sudden disappearance.
  • 比晓，人传南湖有一尸抱一尸浮出 - '比晓' means 'by dawn' or 'in the morning,' and '传' means 'spread' or 'rumor.' This phrase describes a rumor that spread in the morning about a body floating in South Lake, holding another body.
  • 归黑，坐桥上，谓其家卧榻上，仰卧，堕磕桥下石。 - '归黑' means 'returned in the dark,' and this phrase describes the servant returning home late at night, sitting on a bridge, and falling asleep, only to fall off the bridge and hit a stone.
  • 故其死脑碎。 - Means 'thus, he died with a crushed brain,' indicating the cause of death.
  • 此奴醉后坐步口，为鬼拽入水中，教相与抱出。 - '此奴' means 'this servant,' and this phrase suggests that the servant was dragged into the water by a ghost after getting drunk, and was then pulled out by someone else.
  • 要之，任仆之死，人不知其所以死。 - '要之' means 'in summary' or 'in conclusion,' and this phrase indicates that the cause of the servant's death remains unknown.
  • 至孔仆之死，即彼亦不自知其死也，而且父子同死。 - '至' means 'as for,' and this phrase indicates that the death of another servant named '孔' was also unknown, and both father and son died.
  • 天之报恶人，诛叛主贼，亦太奇已。 - '天之报恶人' means 'heaven's punishment for evil people,' and '诛叛主贼' means 'punishing traitors and thieves.' This phrase suggests that heaven punishes evil people in strange ways.
  • 可畏哉！可畏哉！ - An exclamation expressing fear or awe.

  • 南湖 - A specific location, possibly a lake in the area where the story takes place.
  • 水鬼 - Refers to a water ghost, a supernatural being in Chinese folklore.
  • 叛主贼 - Refers to traitors and thieves, indicating a moral judgment on the characters.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '而任为夜不收数年，以罪除粮' describes the servant's role and punishment, with a clear cause-and-effect relationship.
  • The sentence '日纵博大嚼，靴笠偿酒家资，无所投，日拥败絮，空腹卧榻上，无面出见人' describes the servant's lifestyle and state of poverty, with a series of actions and conditions.
  • The sentence '一日偶出门不归，比晓，人传南湖有一尸抱一尸浮出' describes the servant's disappearance and the subsequent rumor, with a clear temporal sequence.
  • The sentence '或曰：'此仆不能忍冻饿，自投水死。'' presents a possible explanation for the servant's death, with a clear attribution.
  • The sentence '或曰：'此仆行遇一故酒徒，饮得醉。'' presents another possible explanation for the servant's death, with a clear attribution.
  • The sentence '归黑，坐桥上，谓其家卧榻上，仰卧，堕磕桥下石。' describes the servant's actions and the cause of his death, with a clear sequence of events.
  • The sentence '故其死脑碎。' concludes the explanation of the servant's death, with a clear cause.
  • The sentence '或曰：'人有堕水死者，其魂常为水鬼，必得代方已。'' presents a belief about the fate of drowning victims, with a clear attribution.
  • The sentence '此奴醉后坐步口，为鬼拽入水中，教相与抱出。' describes the servant's actions and the involvement of a ghost, with a clear sequence of events.
  • The sentence '要之，任仆之死，人不知其所以死。' summarizes the uncertainty surrounding the servant's death, with a clear conclusion.
  • The sentence '至孔仆之死，即彼亦不自知其死也，而且父子同死。' compares the death of another servant, with a clear attribution and additional information.
  • The sentence '天之报恶人，诛叛主贼，亦太奇已。' expresses a moral judgment and a sense of awe, with a clear attribution.
  • The exclamation '可畏哉！可畏哉！' expresses fear or awe, with a clear emotional tone.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 未闻其曾至南海也 - Formal way to express doubt or lack of knowledge, meaning 'I have never heard that he had ever been to the South Sea.'
  • 何由为此间所崇祀 - Rhetorical question, meaning 'How could he be revered here?'
  • 昔鲁人有浮海而失津者 - Historical anecdote, meaning 'Once upon a time, a man from Lu got lost at sea.'
  • 见仲尼与七十二弟子游于海中 - Narrative, meaning 'He saw Confucius and his seventy-two disciples playing in the sea.'
  • 与鲁人木杖，令闭目乘之归 - Action, meaning 'He gave a wooden staff to the man from Lu, instructing him to close his eyes and ride it back.'
  • 告鲁侯筑城以备寇 - Action, meaning 'He told the Duke of Lu to build a city to defend against enemies.'
  • 投其杖，乃龙也 - Transformation, meaning 'When the man threw the staff, it turned into a dragon.'
  • 具以状告鲁侯 - Action, meaning 'He reported the whole story to the Duke of Lu.'
  • 俄而有群燕数万，衔土培城 - Miraculous event, meaning 'Soon after, tens of thousands of swallows brought earth to strengthen the city walls.'
  • 鲁人乃大城曲阜 - Action, meaning 'The people of Lu then built a large city at Qufu.'
  • 既毕，而齐寇至，攻城不克而还 - Sequence of events, meaning 'After it was completed, the Qi invaders came, but failed to capture the city and returned.'
  • 樊迟从来好利 - Statement, meaning 'Fan Chi was always interested in profit.'
  • 乃至此地贩术棉，以给衣食 - Action, meaning 'He came here to trade cotton, providing himself with clothing and food.'
  • 其后更贩至口外地方，与易皮裘，来吴售之，获利至巨万 - Sequence of actions, meaning 'Later, he traded further to the outer regions, exchanging for fur coats, which he sold in Wu, making enormous profits.'
  • 后值吾邑大饥，樊述辄以粟来赈济，饥民始赖以全活 - Sequence of events, meaning 'Later, when our county suffered a great famine, Fan Chi brought grain to relieve the famine, and the starving people were able to survive thanks to him.'
  • 及其没，邑人思之，故祠祀至今未绝也 - Conclusion, meaning 'After his death, the people of the county thought of him, and so his worship continues to this day.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 樊迟 - A disciple of Confucius, known for his interest in practical matters.
  • 孔子 - Confucius, a revered philosopher and sage in Chinese history.
  • 鲁人 - People from the state of Lu, an important region during the Spring and Autumn period.
  • 曲阜 - The capital of the state of Lu, where Confucius lived and taught.
  • 齐寇 - Invaders from the state of Qi, a neighboring state of Lu.
  • 术棉 - Cotton, a valuable commodity in ancient China.
  • 皮裘 - Fur coats, another valuable commodity.
  • 吴 - A state in ancient China, known for its silk and other goods.

Proper Nouns

  • 樊迟 - Fan Chi, a disciple of Confucius.
  • 孔子 - Confucius, a revered philosopher and sage.
  • 鲁人 - People from the state of Lu.
  • 曲阜 - Qufu, the capital of the state of Lu.
  • 齐寇 - Invaders from the state of Qi.
  • 术棉 - Cotton.
  • 皮裘 - Fur coats.
  • 吴 - Wu, a state in ancient China.

[206]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 顧謂 - Compound verb meaning 'to look back and say,' indicating a casual or reflective tone.
  • 汝聞之乎 - Polite way of asking 'have you heard of this?' with '汝' as the second-person pronoun.
  • 血食一方 - Refers to being worshipped in a specific region, with '血食' meaning to be offered sacrifices.
  • 樊遲販棉花 - Humorous reference to the disciple Fan Chi, implying he is engaged in a lowly trade like selling cotton.
  • 厲鬼 - Refers to a vengeful ghost, often associated with those who died unjustly.
  • 流俗 - Refers to the common people or prevailing customs.
  • 黑面虬須 - Describes a dark-faced person with curly beard, likely referring to a deity or figure with a fearsome appearance.
  • 何地無之耶 - Rhetorical question meaning 'is there no such place anywhere?'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 樊遲 - Disciple of Confucius, known for his practical interests rather than philosophical ones.
  • 張公 - Likely refers to Zhang Xun, a general during the Tang Dynasty who resisted the An Lushan Rebellion and was later deified.
  • 雎陽 - Refers to Suizhou, a city in Henan Province, where Zhang Xun fought.
  • 顏魯公 - Refers to Yan Zhenqing, a famous calligrapher and loyal minister during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 城隍廟 - Temple dedicated to the city god, a common feature in Chinese cities.
  • 伍髭須、杜十姨 - Likely local deities or figures, possibly from folklore or regional traditions.

Proper Nouns

  • 古春 - Name of the person being addressed by the narrator.
  • 樊遲 - Name of Confucius' disciple.
  • 張公 - Refers to Zhang Xun, a historical figure.
  • 雎陽 - Name of a city in Henan Province.
  • 顏魯公 - Refers to Yan Zhenqing, a famous calligrapher and loyal minister.
  • 城隍廟 - Name of a type of temple dedicated to the city god.
  • 伍髭須、杜十姨 - Names of local deities or figures.

[207]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 自缢 - A method of suicide by hanging, recognized in ancient China.
  • 披發 - Describes a state of disheveled hair, often associated with distress or death.
  • 白沫 - Describes the foam that can come from the mouth in a state of unconsciousness or death.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 賈人 - Refers to a merchant or trader, indicating a social and economic role.
  • 阿福 - A common name for an adoptive daughter-in-law, indicating a specific social role.
  • 次子 - Refers to the second son, indicating a family hierarchy.
  • 眾客 - Refers to guests at a social gathering, indicating a social context.

Proper Nouns

  • 施氏 - The family or individual being described.
  • 阿福 - The name of the adoptive daughter-in-law.
  • 次子 - The name of the second son (with an unknown name).
  • 鄰某 - The name of the neighbor (with an unknown name).

[208]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 又余蒋氏表弟妇张氏者 - Introduces a new subject in a narrative context, indicating a shift in focus.
  • 少时性颇刚 - Describes the character's personality in her younger years.
  • 后得颠疾 - Refers to a mental disorder, likely epilepsy or a similar condition.
  • 疗治经年乃愈 - Indicates a prolonged period of recovery.
  • 且更柔婉 - Describes a change in her personality after recovery.
  • 好奉佛 - Indicates a religious inclination.
  • 于是举家相爱 - Suggests a positive family dynamic.
  • 然年逾四十，自缢已两次矣 - Highlights a tragic aspect of her life.
  • 又数年 - Indicates a passage of time.
  • 其家将祀神 - Describes a religious activity.
  • 予表弟入索香烛 - Describes an action taken during the worship.
  • 适仆妾皆不在 - Indicates an unusual absence of household staff.
  • 张氏请至佛箩中取之 - Describes her request and action.
  • 遂自上楼去，良久寂然 - Describes her departure and the resulting silence.
  • 予表弟不耐久俟，走视之，则已缢于梁上而死 - Describes the tragic discovery.
  • 后余从姊为余言 - Indicates a second-hand account.
  • 半年前，似尝言每行时，辄有四人相随 - Describes a supernatural experience.
  • 中一美妇人，衣紫绫袄，皂半臂 - Describes the appearance of one of the accompanying figures.
  • 常顾而笑 - Describes the woman's behavior.
  • 其前一人须发皓白，方袍幅巾，似庙中所供土地像者 - Describes another figure.
  • 其后二人须发亦苍，似五六十岁人 - Describes the remaining figures.
  • 三人间或不见，此妇辄引入一洞户 - Describes a mysterious event.
  • 比醒，始知已就缢 - Describes the aftermath of the event.
  • 今竟不免 - Concludes the tragic story.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 颠疾 - Refers to a mental illness, likely epilepsy or a similar condition.
  • 奉佛 - Refers to the practice of Buddhism, indicating a religious inclination.
  • 祀神 - Refers to the worship of gods, a common religious practice in traditional Chinese culture.
  • 佛箩 - Refers to a basket or container used to hold Buddhist relics or items.
  • 土地像 - Refers to the image of a land god, a deity in Chinese folk religion.
  • 自缢 - Refers to the act of hanging oneself, a form of suicide.

Proper Nouns

  • 蒋氏 - The surname of the cousin.
  • 张氏 - The surname of the cousin's wife.
  • 余 - The narrator, likely the author.
  • 予表弟 - The narrator's cousin.
  • 余从姊 - The narrator's elder sister.
